docs.title: Fraggle
docs.text: Fraggle is a Kotlin-based AI assistant framework that integrates with messaging platforms. It connects to local or cloud LLM providers and provides an extensible skill system for tasks like file operations, web fetching, and command execution.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text: - :material-chat-processing: **Chat Bridge Integration**   Connect to messaging platforms like Signal, with support for both direct and group conversations.  - :material-robot: **ReAct Agent Loop**   Iterative reasoning and acting pattern for reliable tool use with any compatible LLM.  - :material-puzzle: **Extensible Skills**   DSL-based skill definitions make it easy to add custom capabilities.  - :material-memory: **Persistent Memory**   Hierarchical memory system with global, chat, and user scopes.  - :material-shield-check: **Sandboxed Execution**   File and shell operations run in a configurable sandbox environment.  - :material-server: **Multiple LLM Providers**   Works with LM Studio, OpenAI, and Anthropic APIs.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Download latest release
wget https://github.com/DrewCarlson/Fraggle/releases/latest/download/fraggle.zip
unzip fraggle.zip
cd fraggle

# Run the configuration wizard
./bin/fraggle configure

# Start chatting
./bin/fraggle chat

See the Getting Started guide for full setup instructions.

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
Fraggle/
├── app/                    # CLI application entry point
├── fraggle/                # Core agent, provider, and skill abstractions
├── fraggle-signal/         # Signal messaging integration
├── fraggle-skills/         # Built-in skill implementations
├── backend/                # REST API server and dashboard
├── shared/                 # Shared models (Kotlin Multiplatform)
└── config/                 # Example configuration files

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Message Received - A message arrives via a chat bridge (or interactive mode)
  2. Agent Processing - The ReAct loop evaluates the message and available tools
  3. Skill Execution - The agent calls skills as needed (file ops, web fetch, etc.)
  4. Response Sent - The final response is delivered back to the user

The agent continues reasoning and acting until it produces a final response or reaches the iteration limit.

docs.title: License
docs.text: Fraggle is published under the MIT License.

docs.title: Agent System
docs.text: The FraggleAgent implements a ReAct-style (Reasoning + Acting) loop for reliable tool use with LLMs.

docs.title: Iteration Limits
docs.text:
The agent has a configurable maximum iteration count (max_iterations) to prevent infinite loops. Each tool call counts as one iteration.

fraggle:
  agent:
    max_iterations: 10  # Default

When the limit is reached, the agent forces a final response from the LLM.

docs.title: System Prompt Construction
docs.text:
The system prompt is built from multiple sources:

  1. SYSTEM.md - Core instructions and capabilities
  2. IDENTITY.md - Agent personality and behavior
  3. USER.md - User-specific instructions
  4. Tool Definitions - Auto-generated from skill registry
  5. Memory Context - Relevant memories from storage

Files are loaded from prompts_dir and concatenated with separators.

docs.title: Conversation History
docs.text:
The agent maintains conversation history for context:

fraggle:
  agent:
    max_history_messages: 20  # Messages to include

History is pruned to fit within the context window while preserving recent exchanges.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
When a skill execution fails:

  1. The error is formatted as a tool result
  2. The LLM receives the error and can retry or adapt
  3. After repeated failures, the agent may explain the issue to the user

docs.title: Temperature and Sampling
docs.text:
Control LLM response randomness:

fraggle:
  agent:
    temperature: 0.7    # 0.0 = deterministic, 2.0 = very random
    max_tokens: 4096    # Maximum response length

docs.title: Context Structure
docs.text:
Each LLM request includes:

{
  "messages": [
    {"role": "system", "content": "...system prompt..."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Previous user message"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "Previous response"},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Current message"}
  ],
  "tools": [
    {
      "name": "read_file",
      "description": "Read the contents of a file...",
      "parameters": {...}
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Tool Result Format
docs.text:
When a tool is executed, the result is added to the conversation:

{
  "role": "tool",
  "tool_call_id": "call_abc123",
  "content": "File contents here..."
}

The LLM then continues reasoning with the tool result in context.

docs.title: Attachments
docs.text:
Some skills (like send_image and screenshot_page) produce attachments rather than text responses. These are handled specially:

  1. Skill returns SkillResult.ImageAttachment
  2. Agent extracts the attachment data
  3. Bridge sends the attachment alongside the text response

docs.title: Provider Compatibility
docs.text:
The agent adapts its behavior based on the LLM provider:

  • OpenAI/LM Studio - Uses OpenAI function calling format
  • Anthropic - Uses Claude tool use format

The core loop remains the same; only the API format changes.

docs.title: Memory System
docs.text: Fraggle includes a hierarchical memory system that persists information across conversations.

docs.title: Memory Scopes
docs.text: Memory is organized into three scopes:

docs.title: Global Memory
docs.text:
Shared across all conversations and users. Useful for:

  • General knowledge the agent should always have
  • Configuration or preferences that apply universally
  • Facts learned that benefit all users

Storage path: $FRAGGLE_ROOT/data/memory/global/

docs.title: Chat Memory
docs.text:
Per-conversation memory. Useful for:

  • Context about the specific chat or group
  • Ongoing project information
  • Chat-specific preferences

Storage path: $FRAGGLE_ROOT/data/memory/chats/{chat_id}/

docs.title: User Memory
docs.text:
Per-user memory that persists across chats. Useful for:

  • User preferences and settings
  • Personal information the user has shared
  • History of user-specific interactions

Storage path: $FRAGGLE_ROOT/data/memory/users/{user_id}/

docs.title: Storage Format
docs.text:
Memories are stored as human-readable markdown files:

data/memory/
├── global/
│   └── general.md
├── chats/
│   └── group-abc123/
│       └── project.md
└── users/
    └── +1234567890/
        └── preferences.md

docs.title: File Structure
docs.text:
Each memory file uses markdown format:

# Project Notes

## Current Sprint
- Working on feature X
- Blocked on dependency Y

## Team Members
- Alice: Frontend
- Bob: Backend

---
Last updated: 2024-01-15

docs.title: Memory Operations
docs.text: The agent can interact with memory through dedicated skills (when enabled):

docs.title: Reading Memory
docs.text: The agent automatically retrieves relevant memory when building context for responses.

docs.title: Writing Memory
docs.text:
The agent can store new information when asked to remember something:

User: Remember that my favorite color is blue.
Agent: I'll remember that your favorite color is blue.

docs.title: Memory in Context
docs.text:
Relevant memories are included in the system prompt, giving the agent context about:

  • Previous conversations
  • User preferences
  • Ongoing projects or tasks

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
fraggle:
  memory:
    base_dir: ./data/memory
Option Description Default base_dir Directory where memory files stored ./data/memory

docs.title: Memory Limits
docs.text:
To prevent context overflow, memory is summarized or truncated:

  • Recent memories are prioritized
  • Large memory files are summarized
  • Per-scope limits prevent any single scope from dominating

docs.title: Personal Assistant
docs.text: Store user preferences: - Preferred name/pronouns - Time zone - Communication style preferences

docs.title: Team Collaboration
docs.text: Store project context: - Current goals and tasks - Team member roles - Decision history

docs.title: Knowledge Base
docs.text: Store reference information: - Documentation links - Common procedures - FAQ answers

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Be Specific - Store specific facts, not vague summaries
  2. Update Regularly - Replace outdated information
  3. Scope Appropriately - Use the right scope for the information
  4. Review Periodically - Memory files can be manually edited

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text: Fraggle is built as a modular Kotlin application with clear separation of concerns.

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
Fraggle/
├── app/                    # CLI application and entry point
├── fraggle/                # Core framework
│   ├── agent/              # ReAct agent implementation
│   ├── provider/           # LLM provider abstractions
│   ├── skill/              # Skill system and DSL
│   └── sandbox/            # Execution sandbox
├── fraggle-signal/         # Signal messenger bridge
├── fraggle-skills/         # Built-in skill implementations
├── shared/                 # Shared models (Kotlin Multiplatform)
├── backend/                # REST API server
├── dashboard/              # Web dashboard (Compose for Web)
└── documented-*            # Annotation processor for config docs

docs.title: FraggleAgent
docs.text:
The central component that orchestrates message processing. It implements a ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) loop:

  1. Receives a message
  2. Sends message + history + tools to LLM
  3. If LLM requests a tool call, executes it and loops back to step 2
  4. If LLM produces a final response, returns it

See Agent System for details.

docs.title: LLM Providers
docs.text:
Abstraction layer for different LLM backends:

  • LM Studio - Local inference via OpenAI-compatible API
  • OpenAI - GPT models via OpenAI API
  • Anthropic - Claude models via Anthropic API

All providers implement the same interface, making it easy to switch between them.

docs.title: Skill System
docs.text:
Skills are the tools available to the agent. They're defined using a Kotlin DSL:

skill("my_skill") {
    description = "Does something useful"

    parameter<String>("input") {
        description = "The input value"
        required = true
    }

    execute { params ->
        val input = params.get<String>("input")
        SkillResult.Success("Result: $input")
    }
}

See Skills for the full skill reference.

docs.title: Memory System
docs.text:
Hierarchical memory storage with three scopes:

  • Global - Shared across all conversations
  • Chat - Per-conversation memory
  • User - Per-user memory across chats

Memory is persisted as human-readable markdown files.

See Memory for details.

docs.title: Sandbox
docs.text:
Execution environment for potentially dangerous operations:

  • File system access
  • Shell command execution
  • Network requests

The sandbox can be configured for different security levels.

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│    Chat     │────>│   Agent     │────>│   Skills    │
│   Bridge    │<────│   (ReAct)   │<────│             │
└─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘
                          │
                          v
                    ┌─────────────┐
                    │   Memory    │
                    └─────────────┘
  1. Chat Bridge receives messages and passes them to the agent
  2. Agent processes messages using the ReAct loop, calling skills as needed
  3. Skills execute operations and return results
  4. Memory stores and retrieves conversation context
  5. Agent generates final response
  6. Chat Bridge sends the response back to the user

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Configuration is managed through a YAML file with type-safe Kotlin models:

@Serializable
data class FraggleConfig(
    val fraggle: FraggleSettings,
)

The @Documented annotation generates runtime documentation that powers the web dashboard's settings UI.

docs.title: Platform Support
docs.text:
Fraggle uses Kotlin Multiplatform for shared code:

  • JVM - Main application, chat bridges, skills
  • JS - Web dashboard (Compose for Web)

Shared modules (like configuration models) compile to both targets.

docs.title: Skills
docs.text: Skills are the tools available to the Fraggle agent. They enable the agent to interact with the file system, web, shell, and more.

docs.title: Built-in Skills
docs.text: Fraggle includes several skill groups out of the box.

docs.title: Filesystem Skills
docs.text:
File operations within the sandbox:

Skill Description Parameters read_file Read file contents path (required), max_lines (default: 1000) write_file Write content to a file path (required), content (required) append_file Append content to a file path (required), content (required) list_files List directory contents path (required), recursive (default: false) search_files Search for files by pattern path (required), pattern (required, supports * and ? wildcards) file_exists Check if a file or directory exists path (required) delete_file Delete a file path (required)

docs.title: Web Skills
docs.text:
Web fetching and browser automation:

Skill Description Parameters fetch_webpage Fetch content from a webpage url (required) fetch_api Fetch data from an API endpoint url (required), method (default: GET) send_image Download and send an image url (required), caption (optional) screenshot_page Take a screenshot of a webpage url (required), full_page (default: false), caption (optional)

Playwright Skills

The fetch_webpage skill uses Playwright for JavaScript rendering when configured. The screenshot_page skill requires Playwright configuration.

docs.title: Playwright Setup
docs.text:
For JavaScript-heavy websites, configure Playwright:

Option 1: Browserless (Docker) 

docker run -p 3000:3000 browserless/chrome

Option 2: Playwright Server 

npx playwright run-server --port 3000

Configuration: 

fraggle:
  web:
    playwright:
      ws_endpoint: ws://localhost:3000
      navigation_timeout: 30000
      wait_after_load: 2000

docs.title: Shell Skills
docs.text:
Command execution in the sandbox:

Skill Description Parameters execute_command Execute a shell command command (required), timeout_seconds (default: 30)

Security

Shell execution includes basic safeguards against dangerous commands (like rm -rf /), but the sandbox configuration determines the actual security level.

docs.title: Scheduling Skills
docs.text:
Task scheduling for deferred operations:

Skill Description Parameters schedule_task Schedule a task for later name, action, delay_seconds, repeat_interval_seconds (optional) list_tasks List all scheduled tasks (none) get_task Get task details task_id cancel_task Cancel a scheduled task task_id

docs.title: Skill DSL
docs.text:
Skills are defined using a Kotlin DSL:

skill("my_skill") {
    description = "A description of what this skill does"

    parameter<String>("input") {
        description = "Description of this parameter"
        required = true
    }

    parameter<Int>("count") {
        description = "Optional count parameter"
        default = 10
    }

    execute { params ->
        val input = params.get<String>("input")
        val count = params.getOrDefault("count", 10)

        // Do work...

        SkillResult.Success("Result message")
    }
}

docs.title: Parameter Types
docs.text:
Supported parameter types:

  • String - Text values
  • Int - Integer numbers
  • Long - Long integers
  • Double - Floating point numbers
  • Boolean - True/false values

docs.title: Skill Results
docs.text:
Skills return one of these result types:

// Success with text output
SkillResult.Success("Operation completed")

// Error with message
SkillResult.Error("Something went wrong")

// Image attachment (for send_image, screenshot_page)
SkillResult.ImageAttachment(
    imageData = byteArray,
    mimeType = "image/png",
    caption = "Optional caption",
    output = "Description of what was done"
)

docs.title: Skill Registry
docs.text:
Skills are organized into a registry with optional grouping:

val registry = SkillRegistry {
    // Ungrouped skills
    install(mySkill)

    // Grouped skills
    group("filesystem", "File system operations") {
        install(readFile)
        install(writeFile)
    }
}

docs.title: Default Registry
docs.text:
The DefaultSkills object provides factory methods:

// Full registry with all skills
val registry = DefaultSkills.createRegistry(
    sandbox = sandbox,
    taskScheduler = taskScheduler,
    playwrightFetcher = playwrightFetcher  // optional
)

// Minimal registry (file + web only)
val registry = DefaultSkills.createMinimalRegistry(sandbox)

// Custom selection
val registry = DefaultSkills.createCustomRegistry(
    sandbox = sandbox,
    includeFile = true,
    includeWeb = true,
    includeShell = false,
    includeScheduling = false
)

docs.title: Custom Skills
docs.text:
Add custom skills by implementing the Skill interface or using the DSL:

// Using the DSL
val weatherSkill = skill("get_weather") {
    description = "Get current weather for a location"

    parameter<String>("location") {
        description = "City name or coordinates"
        required = true
    }

    execute { params ->
        val location = params.get<String>("location")
        // Call weather API...
        SkillResult.Success("Weather in $location: Sunny, 72°F")
    }
}

// Add to registry
val registry = SkillRegistry {
    DefaultSkills.createRegistry(sandbox).skills.forEach { install(it) }
    install(weatherSkill)
}

docs.title: Execution Context
docs.text:
Skills receive execution context with information about the current request:

execute { params ->
    val chatId = params.context?.chatId
    val userId = params.context?.userId

    // Use context for chat-specific behavior
    SkillResult.Success("Response for chat $chatId")
}

This context is used by skills like schedule_task to know which chat to send scheduled messages to.

docs.title: Building
docs.text:
This guide covers building Fraggle from source for development purposes.

For Users

If you just want to run Fraggle, download the latest release instead of building from source. See the Getting Started guide.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• JDK 21+ - Required for compilation and runtime
  • Gradle - Wrapper included (no separate installation needed)

docs.title: Build All Modules
docs.text:
./gradlew build

This compiles all modules and runs tests.

docs.title: Build Without Tests
docs.text: ./gradlew build -x test

docs.title: Build Specific Module
docs.text:
# Core framework
./gradlew :fraggle:build

# Signal integration
./gradlew :fraggle-signal:build

# Built-in skills
./gradlew :fraggle-skills:build

# CLI application
./gradlew :app:build

docs.title: Create Runnable Distribution
docs.text:
./gradlew :app:installDist

Creates a runnable distribution at app/build/install/app/.

Run with: 

./app/build/install/app/bin/app run

docs.title: Create Distribution Archives
docs.text:
./gradlew :app:distZip
./gradlew :app:distTar

Creates archives at app/build/distributions/.

docs.title: Build Fat JAR
docs.text:
./gradlew :app:shadowJar

Creates an executable JAR with all dependencies at app/build/libs/app-all.jar.

Run with: 

java -jar app/build/libs/app-all.jar run

docs.title: Development Mode
docs.text:
When working on Fraggle itself, use the Gradle run task which configures FRAGGLE_ROOT for development:

# Clone the repo
git clone https://github.com/DrewCarlson/Fraggle.git
cd Fraggle

# Set up dev config
mkdir -p runtime-dev/config
cp config/fraggle.yaml runtime-dev/config/fraggle.yaml

# Run in dev mode (sets FRAGGLE_ROOT=runtime-dev/)
./gradlew :app:run --args="chat"

docs.title: Dashboard Build
docs.text:
The web dashboard uses Kotlin/JS with Compose for Web:

# Development build
./gradlew :dashboard:jsBrowserDevelopmentWebpack

# Production build
./gradlew :dashboard:jsBrowserProductionWebpack

Output is at dashboard/build/dist/js/productionExecutable/.

docs.title: Clean Build
docs.text:
# Clean all build outputs
./gradlew clean

# Clean and rebuild
./gradlew clean build

docs.title: Dependency Updates
docs.text:
Check for dependency updates:

./gradlew dependencyUpdates

docs.title: IntelliJ IDEA
docs.text:
1. Open the project directory in IntelliJ
  2. IntelliJ should automatically detect the Gradle project
  3. Wait for the Gradle sync to complete
  4. Use the Gradle tool window for build tasks

docs.title: VS Code
docs.text:
1. Install the Kotlin and Gradle extensions
  2. Open the project directory
  3. Use the Gradle extension for build tasks

docs.title: "Unsupported class file major version"
docs.text:
You're using a JDK version lower than 21. Check with: 

java -version

docs.title: Out of Memory
docs.text:
Increase Gradle's heap size in gradle.properties: 

org.gradle.jvmargs=-Xmx4g

docs.title: Gradle Daemon Issues
docs.text:
Kill existing daemons: 

./gradlew --stop

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to Fraggle.

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository 

    git clone https://github.com/DrewCarlson/Fraggle.git
cd Fraggle

  2. Verify the build works 

    ./gradlew build

  3. Set up your IDE

  4. IntelliJ IDEA is recommended

  5. Open the project directory and let it sync

  6. Create a development config 

    mkdir -p runtime-dev/config
cp config/fraggle.yaml runtime-dev/config/fraggle.yaml

  7. Run in development mode 

    ./gradlew :app:run --args="chat"

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text:
The project follows standard Kotlin coding conventions:

  • Use 4 spaces for indentation
  • Follow Kotlin coding conventions
  • Keep functions focused and small
  • Write descriptive names for classes, functions, and variables
  • Document public APIs with KDoc

docs.title: 1. Create a Branch
docs.text:
git checkout -b feature/my-feature
# or
git checkout -b fix/bug-description

docs.title: 2. Make Your Changes
docs.text:
• Write code
  • Add/update tests
  • Update documentation if needed

docs.title: 3. Run Tests
docs.text: ./gradlew test

docs.title: 4. Commit
docs.text:
Write clear commit messages:

Add weather skill for fetching forecasts

- Implement WeatherSkill with location parameter
- Add tests for various weather conditions
- Document skill in skills.md

docs.title: 5. Push and Create PR
docs.text:
git push origin feature/my-feature

Then create a pull request on GitHub.

docs.title: Pull Request Guidelines
docs.text:
• Provide a clear description of the changes
  • Reference any related issues
  • Ensure tests pass
  • Keep PRs focused - one feature/fix per PR
  • Be responsive to feedback

docs.title: Good First Issues
docs.text: Look for issues labeled good first issue on GitHub.

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Add new skills for:

  • External APIs (weather, news, etc.)
  • System operations
  • Integrations with other services

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Improve existing docs
  • Add examples
  • Fix typos

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
• Increase test coverage
  • Add integration tests
  • Improve test utilities

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Profile and optimize hot paths
  • Reduce memory usage
  • Improve startup time

docs.title: Adding a New Skill
docs.text:
1. Create skill in fraggle-skills/src/main/kotlin/
  2. Add to appropriate skill group (or create new group)
  3. Register in DefaultSkills.kt
  4. Add tests
  5. Document in docs/architecture/skills.md

docs.title: Adding a New Provider
docs.text:
1. Implement LLMProvider interface
  2. Add to provider factory
  3. Add configuration options
  4. Add tests
  5. Document setup

docs.title: Adding a New Bridge
docs.text:
1. Implement bridge interface
  2. Add configuration in ConfigModels.kt
  3. Wire up in service orchestrator
  4. Add documentation

docs.title: Questions?
docs.text:
• Open an issue for questions or discussion
  • Check existing issues before opening new ones
  • Be patient and respectful in discussions

docs.title: License
docs.text: By contributing, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under the MIT License.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text: This guide covers running and writing tests for Fraggle.

docs.title: All Tests
docs.text: ./gradlew test

docs.title: Specific Module
docs.text:
./gradlew :fraggle:test
./gradlew :fraggle-skills:test
./gradlew :fraggle-signal:test

docs.title: Specific Test Class
docs.text:
./gradlew :fraggle:test --tests="*AgentTest"
./gradlew :fraggle-skills:test --tests="*FileSkillsTest"

docs.title: Specific Test Method
docs.text: ./gradlew :fraggle:test --tests="*AgentTest.testReActLoop"

docs.title: With Detailed Output
docs.text: ./gradlew test --info

docs.title: Test Framework
docs.text:
Tests use:

  • JUnit 5 - Test framework
  • MockK - Kotlin-friendly mocking
  • kotlinx-coroutines-test - Coroutine testing utilities
  • Kotest Assertions - Fluent assertions (optional)

docs.title: Basic Test
docs.text:
class MyTest {
    @Test
    fun `test description`() {
        val result = someFunction()
        assertEquals(expected, result)
    }
}

docs.title: Coroutine Test
docs.text:
class CoroutineTest {
    @Test
    fun `test async operation`() = runTest {
        val result = suspendingFunction()
        assertEquals(expected, result)
    }
}

docs.title: Mocking with MockK
docs.text:
class MockTest {
    private val mockProvider = mockk<LLMProvider>()

    @Test
    fun `test with mock`() = runTest {
        coEvery { mockProvider.complete(any()) } returns "mocked response"

        val agent = FraggleAgent(mockProvider)
        val result = agent.process("input")

        assertEquals("mocked response", result)
        coVerify { mockProvider.complete(any()) }
    }
}

docs.title: Testing Skills
docs.text:
class SkillTest {
    private val sandbox = mockk<Sandbox>()

    @Test
    fun `test read_file skill`() = runTest {
        every { sandbox.readFile(any(), any()) } returns
            SandboxResult.Success("file contents")

        val skill = FileSkills.readFile(sandbox)
        val params = SkillParams(mapOf("path" to "test.txt"))
        val result = skill.execute(params)

        assertTrue(result is SkillResult.Success)
        assertEquals("file contents", (result as SkillResult.Success).output)
    }
}

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text:
Test individual components in isolation:

// Located in src/test/kotlin/
class SkillRegistryTest {
    @Test
    fun `registry should find skill by name`() {
        val skill = skill("test") { execute { SkillResult.Success("ok") } }
        val registry = SkillRegistry { install(skill) }

        assertNotNull(registry.find("test"))
        assertNull(registry.find("nonexistent"))
    }
}

docs.title: Integration Tests
docs.text:
Test component interactions:

class AgentIntegrationTest {
    @Test
    fun `agent should execute skills and return response`() = runTest {
        val provider = FakeLLMProvider()
        val sandbox = PermissiveSandbox()
        val registry = DefaultSkills.createRegistry(sandbox)

        val agent = FraggleAgent(provider, registry)
        val result = agent.process("read file test.txt")

        // Verify the agent called the read_file skill
        assertTrue(result.contains("file contents"))
    }
}

docs.title: Sample Configurations
docs.text:
object TestFixtures {
    val sampleConfig = FraggleConfig(
        fraggle = FraggleSettings(
            provider = ProviderConfig(
                type = ProviderType.LMSTUDIO,
                url = "http://localhost:1234/v1"
            )
        )
    )
}

docs.title: Fake Implementations
docs.text:
class FakeLLMProvider : LLMProvider {
    var responses = mutableListOf<String>()
    var callCount = 0

    override suspend fun complete(request: Request): String {
        return responses.getOrElse(callCount++) { "default response" }
    }
}

docs.title: Coverage
docs.text:
Generate coverage reports:

./gradlew test jacocoTestReport

Reports are at build/reports/jacoco/.

docs.title: Continuous Integration
docs.text:
Tests run automatically on:

  • Pull requests
  • Pushes to main branch

Check .github/workflows/ for CI configuration.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Test behavior, not implementation - Focus on what the code does, not how
  2. Use descriptive test names - Use backticks for readable names: `should return error when file not found`
  3. Arrange-Act-Assert - Structure tests clearly
  4. One assertion per test - When practical, test one thing at a time
  5. Mock external dependencies - Don't make real network calls in unit tests

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Fraggle uses a YAML configuration file located at $FRAGGLE_ROOT/config/fraggle.yaml.

docs.title: Configuration Wizard
docs.text:
The easiest way to configure Fraggle is with the interactive wizard:

./bin/fraggle configure

The wizard offers two modes:

  • Quick setup - Configure essential settings: LLM provider, chat bridge, and API server
  • Full setup - Walk through all configuration sections

If a configuration file already exists, the wizard will use your current values as defaults.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Description Default FRAGGLE_ROOT Root directory for all runtime files (config, data, logs) Current directory

docs.title: Full Configuration Reference
docs.text:
fraggle:
  # LLM Provider settings
  provider:
    type: lmstudio                    # lmstudio, openai, anthropic
    url: http://localhost:1234/v1     # API endpoint
    model: ""                         # Model name (optional for LM Studio)
    api_key: ""                       # Required for OpenAI/Anthropic

  # Chat bridge configurations
  bridges:
    signal:
      enabled: true
      phone: "+1234567890"            # Your Signal phone number
      config_dir: ~/.config/fraggle/signal
      trigger: "@fraggle"             # Prefix to trigger bot in groups
      signal_cli_path: null           # Path to signal-cli (null = use PATH)
      respond_to_direct_messages: true
      show_typing_indicator: true

  # Prompt file configuration
  prompts:
    prompts_dir: ./config/prompts     # Directory for prompt files
    max_file_chars: 20000             # Max chars per prompt file
    auto_create_missing: true         # Auto-create from templates

  # Memory storage settings
  memory:
    base_dir: ./data/memory

  # Sandbox settings for file/shell operations
  sandbox:
    type: permissive                  # permissive, docker, gvisor
    work_dir: ./data/workspace

  # Agent behavior settings
  agent:
    temperature: 0.7                  # LLM sampling temperature (0.0-2.0)
    max_tokens: 4096                  # Max response tokens
    max_iterations: 10                # Max tool-use iterations
    max_history_messages: 20          # Messages to include in context

  # Registered chats with custom settings
  chats:
    registered: []
    # - id: "group-abc123"
    #   name: "Dev Team"
    #   trigger_override: "@bot"
    #   enabled: true

  # Web browsing configuration
  web:
    playwright:                       # Optional Playwright config
      ws_endpoint: ws://localhost:3000
      navigation_timeout: 30000
      wait_after_load: 2000
      viewport_width: 1280
      viewport_height: 720
      user_agent: null                # null = browser default

  # REST API server
  api:
    enabled: false
    host: "0.0.0.0"
    port: 8080
    cors:
      enabled: true
      allowed_origins: []

  # Web dashboard
  dashboard:
    enabled: false
    static_path: null                 # null = use embedded assets

docs.title: Provider
docs.text:
Configures the LLM provider connection.

Option Description Default type Provider type: lmstudio, openai, anthropic lmstudio url API endpoint URL http://localhost:1234/v1 model Model identifier (optional for LM Studio) "" api_key API key for authentication null

docs.title: Bridges
docs.text:
Chat platform integrations. Currently supports Signal.

See Signal Setup for detailed Signal configuration.

docs.title: Prompts
docs.text:
The prompt system uses markdown files for customization:

  • SYSTEM.md - Core system prompt and instructions
  • IDENTITY.md - Agent personality and identity
  • USER.md - User-specific instructions and preferences

Files are loaded from prompts_dir and concatenated to form the full system prompt.

docs.title: Memory
docs.text:
Memory is stored as human-readable markdown files in hierarchical scopes:

  • Global - Shared across all conversations (memory/global/)
  • Chat - Per-conversation memory (memory/chats/{chat_id}/)
  • User - Per-user memory (memory/users/{user_id}/)

docs.title: Sandbox
docs.text:
Controls the execution environment for file and shell operations:

Type Description permissive Direct execution with minimal restrictions docker Execute commands in Docker containers gvisor Execute in gVisor-sandboxed containers

docs.title: Agent
docs.text:
Controls agent behavior during the ReAct loop:

Option Description Default temperature LLM sampling temperature 0.7 max_tokens Maximum response tokens 4096 max_iterations Maximum tool-use cycles per request 10 max_history_messages Messages to include in context 20

docs.title: Registered Chats
docs.text:
Pre-configure specific chats with custom settings:

chats:
  registered:
    - id: "group-abc123"        # Signal group ID
      name: "Dev Team"          # Human-readable name
      trigger_override: "@bot"  # Custom trigger for this chat
      enabled: true             # Enable/disable this chat

docs.title: Playwright (Optional)
docs.text:
For JavaScript-heavy websites, configure Playwright for full browser rendering:

web:
  playwright:
    ws_endpoint: ws://localhost:3000
    navigation_timeout: 30000     # Page load timeout (ms)
    wait_after_load: 2000         # Wait for JS to settle (ms)
    viewport_width: 1280
    viewport_height: 720
    user_agent: null              # Custom user agent

See Skills - Web Skills for setup instructions.

docs.title: API Server
docs.text:
Enable the REST API for external integrations:

api:
  enabled: true
  host: "0.0.0.0"
  port: 8080
  cors:
    enabled: true
    allowed_origins:
      - "http://localhost:3000"
      - "https://myapp.example.com"

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text:
Enable the web dashboard UI (requires API to be enabled):

api:
  enabled: true
dashboard:
  enabled: true
  static_path: null  # Use embedded assets

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide walks you through setting up Fraggle from scratch.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• JDK 21+ - Fraggle requires Java 21 or later
  • LLM Provider - One of:
    • LM Studio (local, recommended for development)
    • OpenAI API key
    • Anthropic API key
  • signal-cli - Optional, for Signal chat bridge (installation guide)

docs.title: 1. Download the Latest Release
docs.text:
wget https://github.com/DrewCarlson/Fraggle/releases/latest/download/fraggle.zip
unzip fraggle.zip
cd fraggle

docs.title: 2. Configure Fraggle
docs.text:
Run the interactive configuration wizard:

./bin/fraggle configure

This walks you through setting up:

  • LLM provider (LM Studio, OpenAI, or Anthropic)
  • Signal integration (optional)
  • API server and dashboard (optional)

Alternatively, copy and edit the example configuration manually:

cp config/fraggle.example.yaml config/fraggle.yaml

See the Configuration guide for all options.

docs.title: 3. Test with Interactive Mode
docs.text:
Before setting up chat bridges, test that everything works:

./bin/fraggle chat

This starts an interactive chat session where you can test the agent directly.

docs.title: Interactive Chat Mode
docs.text:
Test the agent without chat bridges:

# With default config
./bin/fraggle chat

# With custom config file
./bin/fraggle chat -c /path/to/config.yaml

# With model override
./bin/fraggle chat -m gpt-4

docs.title: Full Service Mode
docs.text:
Run with chat bridges enabled:

# With default config
./bin/fraggle run

# With custom config file
./bin/fraggle run -c /path/to/config.yaml

docs.title: Custom Installation Location
docs.text:
To install Fraggle in a specific location, set FRAGGLE_ROOT:

export FRAGGLE_ROOT=/opt/fraggle
./bin/fraggle run

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
When running, Fraggle creates this directory structure under FRAGGLE_ROOT:

$FRAGGLE_ROOT/
├── config/
│   ├── fraggle.yaml        # Main configuration
│   └── prompts/            # Prompt templates
│       ├── SYSTEM.md       # System prompt
│       ├── IDENTITY.md     # Agent identity/personality
│       └── USER.md         # User-specific instructions
├── data/
│   ├── memory/             # Conversation memory storage
│   └── workspace/          # Sandbox working directory
└── logs/
    └── fraggle.log         # Application logs (daily rotation)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Configuration Reference - Full configuration options
  • Signal Setup - Connect to Signal messenger
  • Skills - Available tools and custom skill development

docs.title: Signal Setup
docs.text: This guide covers setting up Signal integration for Fraggle.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• signal-cli installed
  • A phone number to register with Signal

docs.title: macOS (Homebrew)
docs.text: brew install signal-cli

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
Download the latest release from signal-cli releases:

# Example for version 0.13.0
wget https://github.com/AsamK/signal-cli/releases/download/v0.13.0/signal-cli-0.13.0.tar.gz
tar xf signal-cli-0.13.0.tar.gz
sudo mv signal-cli-0.13.0 /opt/signal-cli
sudo ln -s /opt/signal-cli/bin/signal-cli /usr/local/bin/signal-cli

docs.title: Option 1: New Registration
docs.text:
Register a new phone number with Signal:

# Request verification code via SMS
signal-cli -u +1234567890 register

# Or request via voice call
signal-cli -u +1234567890 register --voice

# Verify with the code you received
signal-cli -u +1234567890 verify CODE

docs.title: Option 2: Link to Existing Account
docs.text:
Link signal-cli as a secondary device to an existing Signal account:

signal-cli link -n "Fraggle Bot"

This displays a QR code or URI. Scan it with the Signal app on your phone:

  1. Open Signal on your phone
  2. Go to Settings > Linked Devices
  3. Tap "Link New Device"
  4. Scan the QR code

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Add Signal configuration to your fraggle.yaml:

fraggle:
  bridges:
    signal:
      enabled: true
      phone: "+1234567890"            # Your registered number
      config_dir: ~/.config/fraggle/signal
      trigger: "@fraggle"             # Trigger for group messages
      respond_to_direct_messages: true
      show_typing_indicator: true

docs.title: Configuration Options
docs.text: Option Description Default phone Registered Signal phone number (with country code) Required enabled Whether Signal bridge is active true if phone is set config_dir Directory for Signal configuration ~/.config/fraggle/signal trigger Prefix to trigger bot in group chats @fraggle signal_cli_path Path to signal-cli (null = use PATH) null respond_to_direct_messages Respond to DMs without trigger true show_typing_indicator Show typing indicator while processing true

docs.title: Direct Messages
docs.text: When respond_to_direct_messages is true, Fraggle responds to all direct messages automatically.

docs.title: Group Messages
docs.text:
In group chats, Fraggle only responds when the message starts with the trigger prefix:

@fraggle what's the weather like?

Set trigger to null to respond to all group messages (use with caution).

docs.title: Per-Chat Triggers
docs.text:
You can override the trigger for specific chats:

fraggle:
  bridges:
    signal:
      trigger: "@fraggle"
  chats:
    registered:
      - id: "group-abc123"
        name: "Dev Team"
        trigger_override: "@bot"    # Use @bot in this group

docs.title: Text Formatting
docs.text:
Fraggle supports Signal's text formatting. The LLM can use markdown-like syntax that's automatically converted:

Syntax Result **bold** Bold *italic* Italic ~~strikethrough~~ ~~Strikethrough~~ \|\|spoiler\|\| Spoiler (hidden until tapped) `monospace` Monospace

Unsupported Formatting

Markdown links ([text](url)) and images (![alt](url)) are NOT supported in Signal messages. Images must be sent as attachments using the send_image skill.

docs.title: "Failed to connect to Signal"
docs.text:
1. Verify signal-cli is installed: signal-cli --version
  2. Check the phone number is registered: signal-cli -u +1234567890 receive
  3. Ensure config_dir exists and is writable

docs.title: "Messages not being received"
docs.text:
1. Run in interactive mode first to verify the agent works: ./gradlew :app:run --args="chat"
  2. Check logs at $FRAGGLE_ROOT/logs/fraggle.log
  3. Try receiving messages manually: signal-cli -u +1234567890 receive

docs.title: "Permission denied" errors
docs.text:
Ensure the user running Fraggle has:

  • Read/write access to config_dir
  • Execute permission on signal-cli

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
• The Signal configuration directory contains sensitive authentication data
  • Restrict access to config_dir (e.g., chmod 700)
  • Consider running Fraggle as a dedicated user
  • The bot can access messages in any chat it's added to - be mindful of group memberships
